proposal.reasoning: We are in the exploration phase with no evaluated properties yet. hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Increasing the dummy‑bead bond lengths (CGD2_DUP_bl, SUL_DUN_bl) separates the positive and negative dummy charges, directly raising the molecular dipole moment. Raising the magnitude of the Lennard‑Jones epsilon (making it more negative) strengthens intermolecular cohesion, which should increase density, heat of vaporization and surface tension. Larger opposite charges on the dummy beads (DUP_charge / DUN_charge) also boost the dipole while keeping the overall charge neutral. Adjusting bond/angle force constants and core‑core bond length (SUL_bl) tunes packing and flexibility without violating stability constraints.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: Fork 1 should produce a relatively low dipole and lower bulk density, serving as a baseline. Fork 2 is expected to yield a higher dipole (~target 3.96 D) and higher density, heat of vaporization and surface tension, moving the simulation toward the experimental targets. Comparing the two will reveal the sensitivity of dipole‑controlled electronic properties versus LJ‑driven thermodynamic properties.

proposal.reasoning: The diagnostics show low density (under‑packing) together with too‑high surface tension and a dipole moment below target. To raise density we shrink the LJ core size (reduce CGD2_SUL_rmin and SUL_rminby2). Because a smaller core can increase cohesion too much, we adjust the LJ epsilon in opposite directions in the two forks to explore the trade‑off between density and surface tension: Fork 1 makes epsilon more attractive to see how far density can be pushed, while Fork 2 makes epsilon slightly less attractive to temper surface tension. The dipole moment is increased by lengthening the dummy‑core bonds (CGD2_DUP_bl, SUL_DUN_bl) and raising the opposite dummy charges (DUP_charge, DUN_charge). This yields one exploratory, aggressive set (Fork 1) and one moderate, exploitation‑oriented set (Fork 2).

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Reducing CGD2_SUL_rmin (and the derived SUL_rminby2) makes the LJ core smaller, allowing molecules to pack more tightly and thus increasing liquid density. Lengthening the dummy‑core bond lengths (CGD2_DUP_bl, SUL_DUN_bl) increases the spatial separation of opposite charges, and raising the magnitude of the dummy charges (DUP_charge, DUN_charge) directly amplifies the molecular dipole moment. Together these changes should raise the dipole toward the target without destabilising the system.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: Higher density (by tighter packing), a dipole moment closer to the experimental value (by larger charge separation), and a reduction of surface tension (by moderating epsilon in Fork 2).
